title: Wheel tension and take-up systems explained
subtitle: How scotch tension, double-drive, and Irish tension differ, and how to set each for the yarn you want
excerpt: A spinning wheel's tension system controls how quickly twist enters the yarn and how firmly the bobbin draws it on. Scotch tension, double-drive, and Irish tension each work differently, and misreading which system your wheel uses is one of the most common causes of snapping singles and underfed yarn. This reading explains each mechanism and how to adjust it for lace-weight through chunky spinning.
sourceNotes: The mechanics of scotch tension, double-drive, and Irish tension systems are standard wheel-design principles documented across spinning wheel manufacturer manuals and modern spinning texts including Mabel Ross and Judith MacKenzie. The take-up adjustment guidance reflects widely taught practice across fibre guilds.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single-drive wheel setup in which the drive band runs from the wheel to the flyer whorl only, and a separate brake band (the scotch tension spring and cord) applies friction to the bobbin. The bobbin is slowed by the brake band while the flyer turns freely, and this difference in speed draws the yarn onto the bobbin.

A wheel setup in which a single drive band forms a figure-of-eight and turns both the flyer whorl and the bobbin whorl simultaneously. The two whorls are different sizes, so the flyer and bobbin turn at different speeds; the speed difference between them is what draws the spun yarn onto the bobbin.

The rate at which the bobbin draws the spun yarn onto itself as the spinner drafts. Take-up is controlled by the tension system: more tension means faster take-up. Too much take-up breaks the singles or prevents the spinner from drafting; too little leaves the yarn underfed and allows twist to pile up near the orifice.

content.text: Every spinning wheel has a mechanism that does two things at once: it twists the fibre into yarn, and it draws the finished yarn onto a bobbin. These two actions are in constant tension with each other (literally), and adjusting the balance between them is one of the core skills of wheel spinning. The three main systems are
scotch tension
double-drive

, and Irish tension (also called bobbin-lead). Each works by creating a speed difference between the flyer and the bobbin, and understanding which system your wheel uses is the first step to dialling it in.
Scotch tension: the brake-band system

In a scotch tension setup, a single drive band runs from the drive wheel to the flyer whorl only. The flyer turns continuously as you treadle. A separate, lighter brake band wraps around the bobbin and connects via a spring to a tensioning knob or peg. This brake band slows the bobbin slightly while the flyer continues to turn. Because the flyer turns a little faster than the bobbin, each rotation wraps another increment of yarn around the bobbin core.
Turning the brake-band knob tighter increases the friction on the bobbin, which speeds up
take-up

. Loosening it reduces take-up. For a fine lace-weight yarn you want very slow take-up: loosen the brake band until the yarn barely feeds onto the bobbin with each treadle stroke. For a DK or aran weight with more twist per inch, the take-up can be firmer.

The scotch tension system is very responsive to small adjustments and is forgiving for beginners because the brake band can be set very loosely without affecting the flyer rotation. Most Ashford and Louet wheels are scotch tension in their standard configuration.
Double-drive: the figure-of-eight band

In a double-drive setup, one continuous drive band forms a figure-of-eight that loops around the main drive wheel, over the flyer whorl, and back around the bobbin whorl. Both the flyer and the bobbin are driven by the same band, but because the flyer whorl and the bobbin whorl are different diameters, they turn at slightly different speeds. That difference in speed draws the yarn onto the bobbin.

The take-up in a double-drive wheel is adjusted by changing the overall tension of the drive band: a slacker band reduces the speed difference between flyer and bobbin, which reduces take-up. A tighter band increases the difference and increases take-up. The tension is set with the mother-of-all adjustment (the sliding piece that carries the flyer and maiden assembly) rather than a separate brake band.

Double-drive wheels are often described as better suited to very fine spinning because the take-up is gentler and more consistent than a scotch tension brake. They are less immediately adjustable than scotch tension but produce a very smooth feed once dialled in. Schacht Matchless and traditional Norwegian wheels often use double-drive.
Irish tension: bobbin-lead

Irish tension is the reverse of scotch tension. The drive band turns the bobbin directly, and the flyer is controlled by a brake band. Because the bobbin is being driven and the flyer is slowed, the bobbin turns faster than the flyer: the yarn is pulled onto the bobbin more aggressively. This is sometimes called bobbin-lead.

Irish tension suits high-twist, high-take-up work: plying at speed, or spinning bulky yarn where a fast bobbin feed is useful. It is not well-suited to fine lace-weight spinning, where the aggressive take-up is difficult to modulate finely enough. Many wheels do not offer Irish tension as a standard option.
Adjusting take-up for yarn weight
Lace-weight (30+ WPI): very loose take-up. The yarn should resist going onto the bobbin slightly. You should be able to hold the yarn against the pull for a second or two before it feeds on.
Fingering to DK (16-22 WPI): light take-up. The yarn feeds on with a gentle pull at each treadle stroke.
Aran to bulky (6-9 WPI): moderate take-up. The heavier yarn needs more pull to seat onto the bobbin.
What goes wrong with take-up

items.problem: The singles breaks at thin spots near the orifice
items.cause: Take-up is too tight: the bobbin is pulling the yarn on faster than twist can build to support it at thin spots.
items.fix: Loosen the brake band (scotch tension) or slacken the drive band tension (double-drive) slightly. Test by holding the yarn gently at the orifice: you should be able to resist the pull for a count of two without the yarn breaking.

items.problem: Twist piles up at the orifice and the yarn will not feed onto the bobbin
items.cause: Take-up is too loose: the bobbin is not pulling fast enough and the twist has nowhere to go.
items.fix: Tighten the brake band or drive band one small increment at a time. If the yarn still does not feed in, check whether the drive band is seated in the correct whorl groove for the weight of yarn you are spinning.

items.problem: The take-up is inconsistent and the bobbin seems to pull in lurches
items.cause: The drive band is uneven in tension around its loop, or the bobbin whorl groove has a worn patch.
items.fix: Check the drive band for a knot or splice that may be causing a bump each revolution. On a double-drive wheel, a slightly uneven band length creates a lurching pattern: replace the band with a fresh length of drive cord.
